paper_title: Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe by combining two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision. However, how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and \textsc{OPD} use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD} updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers, with the largest relative movement usually appearing in FFN modules. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork nearly recovers full-training performance. The sparse support does not remove the need for adaptive optimization: SGD, previously reported to be competitive in \textsc{RLVR}, underperforms AdamW in our \textsc{OPD} optimizer ablation, suggesting that dense teacher supervision preserves useful momentum structure and heterogeneous second-moment scales. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: On-policy distillation (OPD) has recently become prominent post-training recipe by combining two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision. However, how this hybrid changes models parameters remains unclear. Across several language and visionlanguage model pairs and OPD use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, OPD updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers, with the largest relative movement usually appearing in FFN modules. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork nearly recovers full-training performance. The sparse support does not remove the need for adaptive optimization: SGD, previously reported to be competitive in RLVR, underperforms AdamW in our OPD optimizer ablation, suggesting that dense teacher supervision preserves useful momentum structure and heterogeneous second-moment scales. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn OPD into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, OPD retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training. Code is available at https://github.com/SydCS/OPD-Param-Analysis.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: On-policy distillation (OPD) is emerging as third major component in post-training pipelines for large language models, alongside supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning (Yang et al., 2025; Li et al., 2026; Xiao et al., 2026; DeepSeek-AI, 2026). Recent practice uses OPD to transfer frontier-model reasoning into lightweight students, unify multiple post-training capabilities, or turn privileged information into self-improvement signals (Song & Zheng, 2026). The reason for this momentum is conceptually intuitive. Conventional supervised finetuning and offline distillation provide dense token-level supervision, but train on fixed demonstrations that may differ from what the model will generate at test time, where distribution shift can cause compounding errors (Ross et al., 2011; Bengio et al., 2015). RLVR trains on the current policys own samples, but learns from environmentor verifier-derived outcome rewards that are often sparse, and therefore faces credit-assignment problem. OPD, motivated by on-policy imitation approaches, combines the attractive parts of both: it keeps the data on-policy from the student model while replacing environmental reward feedback with dense teacher supervision (Agarwal et al., 2024). Table 1 summarizes this conceptual position relative to SFT, offline sequence-level distillation, and RLVR. Corresponding authors. 1 Preprint. Table 1: Conceptual position of OPD relative to SFT, offline SEQKD, and RLVR. OPD combines on-policy student samples with dense teacher feedback. Training regime Learning signal Sample source Analogy SFT Offline SEQKD RLVR OPD Fixed data Teacher demonstrations Current policy Current policy Dense token labels Dense token labels Sparse trajectory reward Dense teacher feedback Behavioral Cloning in imitation learning Behavioral Cloning in imitation learning Policy-based reinforcement learning Interactive imitation learning Despite its empirical success, how OPD changes model remains less understood. Prior post-training pipelines were often organized as SFT followed by RLVR, and recent work shows that these two stages can behave very differently in weight space. Mukherjee et al. (2026a) find that densely supervised off-policy fine-tuning produces much denser parameter updates than sparse-reward RLVR, which instead modifies relatively small subnetwork. Zhu et al. (2025) further argue that RLVR moves away from the principal directions of the source weights. These findings leave gap precisely where OPD sits: if the data are on-policy, but the learning signal comes from dense teacher feedback rather than sparse environment reward, should the update resemble supervised fine-tuning, RLVR, or constitute distinct regime? This paper answers this question by analyzing the sparsity and geometry of OPD parameter updates. We compute parameter updates between source models and OPD-trained models, measure their norm and coordinate sparsity, localize their layer and module structure, compare their overlap with RLVR updates, and analyze their singular spectra and alignment with source-weight singular subspaces. Results across several model pairs give coherent picture: OPD-style updates are small in relative norm, sparse at checkpoint precision, usually show the largest relative movement in FFN modules, numerically full-rank yet spectrally concentrated, and strongly biased away from source-principal coordinates toward coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. OPD subnetwork masks also overlap with RLVR or OPD masks obtained with different teachers far above random baselines. We further test whether these signatures matter operationally through two interventions. First, we restart OPD from the source model and train only coordinates selected by nonzero checkpoint-delta masks. The mask discovered from the full OPD run recovers nearly the same reasoning performance as full training, while density-matched random masks generally perform worse. Second, we compare AdamW and SGD under the same OPD setting and inspect AdamW optimizer states. SGD lags behind AdamW, suggesting that sparse final-update support does not by itself make adaptive coordinate-wise scaling unnecessary. Together, these results show that dense teacher supervision does not make OPD an ordinary dense parameter-rewriting process. Although OPD lies between supervised fine-tuning and RLVR in objective design, its parameter updates are closer to sparse on-policy editing than to dense supervised rewriting. This points to the on-policy data distribution, not reward sparsity alone, as major determinant of post-training update geometry.

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.content: 2.1 OPD as On-Policy Dense Supervision OPD can be formulated as dense teacher supervision applied to on-policy student behavior (Agarwal et al., 2024). In ordinary supervised fine-tuning or offline knowledge distillation, the model is optimized on fixed dataset of target sequences (Hinton et al., 2015; Kim & Rush, 2016). In RLVR, the model samples from its current policy and receives an environmentor verifier-derived reward signal, often at the sequence level (Shao et al., 2024). In OPD, the student also samples from its current policy, but the teacher supplies token-level feedback on the sampled sequence. This gives OPD the data distribution of on-policy learning and the dense supervision of distillation. 2 Preprint. Formally, let πθ be the student policy and πT be the teacher. For prompt D, OPD first samples response = (y1, . . . , yy) πθ(x), and then minimizes teacher-student divergence on the student-generated trajectory: LOPD(θ) = xD, yπθ (x) (cid:34) t=1 D(πT(x, y<t) πθ(x, y<t)) , (1) (cid:35) where can be instantiated as forward / reverse KL divergence, JS divergence, or another token-level -divergence. The key point is that the conditioning prefixes y<t come from the student, not from fixed teacher trace. This distinction turns OPD into useful diagnostic for post-training dynamics. If sparse RLVR updates were mainly consequence of sparse environment rewards, then replacing those rewards with dense teacher-derived feedback might be expected to produce much denser parameter changes. If instead the key ingredient is that the model trains near its own policy distribution, then OPD should retain part of the sparse and off-principal structure observed in RLVR, despite changing both the source and granularity of the learning signal. 2.2 Parameter Dynamics in LLM Post-Training Recent work has begun to analyze post-training through parameter updates rather than only through reward curves or benchmark scores. Mukherjee et al. (2026a) show that RLVR often changes only small subnetwork of an LLM, with subnetwork-only fine-tuning recovering the full run. Zhu et al. (2025) argue that this apparent sparsity reflects model-conditioned geometry: RLVR updates are biased away from the principal directions of the source weights and toward low-curvature, spectrum-preserving regions. Mukherjee et al. (2026b) connect these dynamics to optimization, showing that SGD can be surprisingly competitive with AdamW in RLVR and can induce even sparser visible updates. These findings raise the question of what parameter characteristics OPD induces.

sections.title: 3 Analysis of Parameter Updates
sections.content: We use model checkpoint deltas to ask three complementary questions about what OPD changes in weight space. First, scale and coordinate-support metrics ask whether the final update is globally small and whether it is written into many or few visible coordinates. Second, spectral metrics ask whether 2D weight-matrix updates are low-rank in strict sense, or instead full-rank with concentrated singular-value energy. Third, source-geometry metrics ask whether updates reuse the dominant singular directions of the source weights or write into off-principal, low-magnitude coordinates. We examine the parameter updates between source and trained checkpoints across ten model pairs for the scale, sparsity, spectral, and source-geometry analyses in Sections 4 and 5. See Appendix A.3 for details. Six are OPD-style pairs: two DS-Qwen1 OPD runs distilled separately from the DeepScaleR and JustRL teachers (Luo et al., 2025; He et al., 2025), MiniCPM5-1B-SFT to MiniCPM5-1B OPD (OpenBMB, 2026), Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct distilled from the NoisyRollout-7B teacher (Bai et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2026), Qwen3-1.7B-Base distilled from the Qwen3-4B-Base-GRPO teacher (Li et al., 2026), and Qwen3-4B to an OPSD checkpoint (Yang et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2026). We also include three RLVR references: DeepScaleR-1.5B (Luo et al., 2025), JustRL (He et al., 2025), and Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct GRPO checkpoint. Finally, we include Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B to DS-Qwen as conventional distillation-style contrast (Yang et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2025). Together, these analyzed model pairs cover both LLM and VLM settings, as well as the three application categories outlined in Appendix A.1: large-to-small distillation, capability consolidation, and self-distillation from privileged information. Gray bars are reference runs rather than OPD runs: Qwen-Math Distill is an offline distillation-style contrast, and the remaining gray bars are RLVR references. for norms, SVD, projections, and energy statistics. sections.title: 4 Sparsity of OPD Updates
sections.content: 4.1 Global Norm and Coordinate Sparsity OPD-style updates are small relative to their source checkpoints. Across the six OPDstyle pairs, the relative Frobenius norm ranges from 0.036% for Qwen3 OPSD to 0.142% for Qwen2.5-VL OPD. The visible coordinate sparsity is also high: at the 105 threshold, 66.72% to 89.50% of coordinates have no visible movement. The offline distillation contrast behaves very differently, with 11.94% relative delta norm and only 3.06% sparsity at the same threshold. The key takeaway from these numbers is not that every OPD run has the same sparsity level, but that OPD does not become dense simply because of the dense teacher signal. Qwen2.5-VL OPD has the broadest update among the OPD-style checkpoints, yet it still has nearly two orders of magnitude smaller relative norm than the offline distillation contrast 4 Preprint. Table 2: Global delta statistics. Percentages are computed from final checkpoint differences. Sparsity is the percentage of coordinates whose update is numerically indistinguishable from zero at tolerance 105. Top-16 SVD energy and stable rank summarize spectral concentration. Principal and low-magnitude coverage are visible-update coverage scores with 10% source-coordinate masks. Gray text marks non-OPD reference runs. Principal Pair coverage (%) Top-16 SVD Stable rank energy (%) Rel. norm Sparsity (%) Low-mag. coverage (%) (%) Qwen-Math Distill DeepScaleR RLVR DS-Qwen OPD JustRL RLVR DS-Qwen OPD (JustRL) MiniCPM OPD Qwen2.5-VL GRPO Qwen2.5-VL OPD Qwen3-1.7B OPD Qwen3 OPSD 11.936 0.073 0.047 0.497 0.108 0.069 0.045 0.142 0.045 0. 3.06 77.89 82.58 34.68 70.43 81.65 86.56 66.72 79.03 89.50 8.57 19.62 26.92 26.94 40.94 26.81 21.75 30.52 19.69 26.19 82.74 16.07 9.97 12.35 7.25 12.32 16.57 12.58 20.31 7.77 9.95 5.29 4.81 8.34 5.46 5.39 4.58 5.23 4.45 4.39 10.10 31.26 38.04 14.70 23.04 39.42 44.46 24.99 34.69 48.57 and preserves clear coordinate concentration. This suggests that on-policy training can still keep the update local in weight space. JustRL provides useful boundary case for interpreting the RLVR references. Unlike DeepScaleR and Qwen2.5-VL GRPO, the JustRL checkpoint has larger relative delta norm (0.497%), much lower visible sparsity (34.68%), and weaker source-geometry avoidance: its 10% principal-mask coverage is 8.34%, close to the random-density baseline, while its lowmagnitude coverage is only 14.70%. plausible explanation is that the long, single-stage JustRL training process accumulates broader displacement more aligned with sourceprincipal coordinates than shorter or more localized GRPO runs (He et al., 2025). Even so, JustRL remains spectrally concentrated, with 26.94% median top-16 SVD energy and stable rank 12.35, suggesting that densification in coordinate space does not erase the low-dimensional spectral profile. When the same JustRL checkpoint is used as the OPD teacher, the resulting OPD delta returns to the usual OPD-style pattern: small relative norm (0.108%), high sparsity (70.43%), strong spectral concentration, and off-principal source-geometry behavior. This boundary case suggests that the sparsity of the teachers own update is not necessary condition for OPD to produce sparse student update. 4.2 Layer and Module Structure Having established that OPD updates are globally small and sparse, we next ask how this structure is distributed across layers and modules. Figure 2 shows the DS-Qwen OPD sparsity profile by transformer layer and parameter matrix, indicating that OPD update sparsity is distributed across transformer layers rather than being isolated to single layer. The average sparsity decreases from the low-80% range in early layers to around 70% in later layers, but all major projection matrices remain substantially sparse throughout the network. Among individual matrices, oproj stays relatively sparse in later layers, while kproj becomes visibly less sparse near the end of the model. This pattern suggests that OPD does not simply update single layer block; it writes sparse subnetwork across many layers and modules. Table 3 reports the relative delta norm within each module group, WbF/WbF. This metric is the module-level analogue of the global relative norm in Table 2 and is less confounded by the number of parameters in each module. In most OPD-style runs, FFN modules have the largest or near-largest relative movement. However, the module pattern is not universal: MiniCPM OPD has comparable FFN, attention, and LM-head movement, Qwen3-1.7B OPD and Qwen3 OPSD have larger relative movement in attention, and the JustRL RLVR reference is dominated by the LM head. Normalization parameters remain nearly unchanged in all analyzed OPD checkpoints. 5 Preprint. Figure 2: Layerwise and per-parameter-matrix update sparsity for DS-Qwen OPD. Sparsity counts coordinates whose delta between stored checkpoint weights is numerically indistinguishable from zero at tolerance 105. Table 3: Block-level relative delta norm, WbF/WbF, reported as percentage. dash in the LM-head column indicates that no matched standalone lm head delta is available, because the output LM head is tied to the token embedding. The vision-tower column applies to the VLM checkpoint. Pair FFN Attention Embedding LM head Normalization Vision tower 0.1136 DeepScaleR RLVR 0.0741 DS-Qwen OPD JustRL RLVR 0.4643 DS-Qwen OPD (JustRL) 0.1720 0.0763 MiniCPM OPD 0.1629 Qwen2.5-VL OPD 0.0819 Qwen3-1.7B OPD 0.0376 Qwen3 OPSD 0.0326 0.0207 0.3273 0.0487 0.0675 0.1085 0.0934 0. 0.0512 0.0171 0.3365 0.0277 0.0425 0.1121 0.0331 0.0078 0.0594 0.0370 1.6522 0.0586 0.0689 0.0009 0.0002 0.2693 0.0008 0.0000 0.0002 0.0000 0.0001 0.1580 Sparsity and relative norm give complementary views of the same checkpoint delta. The sparsity profile counts how many coordinates in each layer or matrix remain unchanged under the numerical threshold, whereas the module relative norm measures how strongly each module group changes relative to its own source-weight scale. In the present checkpoints, the two views together show that OPD writes sparse updates broadly across layers, while the modules with the largest relative movement vary across models. This module structure suggests that future parameter-efficient OPD designs may need module-aware capacity allocation rather than uniform budget. 4.3 OPD-RLVR Mask Overlap To test whether OPD reuses the same sparse subnetwork as RLVR, we compare visibleupdate masks from checkpoint deltas using one-sided coverage because the mask densities can differ substantially. See Appendix A.5 for metric definitions. Table 4 shows that the observed coverage is roughly three times the corresponding independent-mask baseline. On DS-Qwen, for example, the DeepScaleR RLVR and DSQwen OPD masks cover each other far above random expectation: 53.21% versus 17.50% in the RLVR-to-OPD direction, and 67.20% versus 22.11% in the reverse direction. The same pattern holds in the VLM setting, where the Qwen2.5-VL GRPO mask is much smaller than the OPD mask but 73.53% of its updated coordinates are covered by OPD, compared with 33.28% baseline. It also holds across DS-Qwen OPD runs distilled from different teachers, 6 Preprint. Model family Setting Random Observed Sparsity DS-Qwen DS-Qwen Qwen2.5-VL Qwen2.5-VL DS-Qwen DS-Qwen I1: DeepScaleR RLVR I2: OPD (DeepScaleR teacher) I1: GRPO I2: OPD I1: OPD (DeepScaleR teacher) I2: OPD (JustRL teacher) o1: 17.50 o2: 22.11 o1: 33.28 o2: 13.44 o1: 29.57 o2: 17.50 o1: 53.21 (3.04) o2: 67.20 (3.04) o1: 73.53 (2.21) o2: 29.69 (2.21) o1: 80.17 (2.71) o2: 47.45 (2.71) 77.89 82. 86.56 66.72 82.58 70.43 DeepScaleR and JustRL (Luo et al., 2025; He et al., 2025), where coverage reaches 80.17% in one direction and 47.45% in the other. These overlaps suggest that OPD, RLVR, and teacher-varied OPD do not merely produce sparse updates of the same density; they preferentially touch intersecting coordinates that may be related to models reasoning capability. 4.4 Fine-Tuning the Subnetwork Alone Suffices in OPD The first functional test asks whether the sparse OPD subnetwork is only post-hoc description or whether it is sufficient for training. Given source checkpoint θ0 and full OPD checkpoint θfull, we define the full-OPD mask mi = 1{θfull,i θ0,i > τ}, (2) where τ = 105 after subtracting the stored checkpoint weights. We restart OPD from θ0, use the same teacher and training configuration, zero gradients outside chosen mask, and restore frozen coordinates after each optimizer step so that decoupled weight decay cannot move them. We compare four runs: full OPD, OPD restricted to the full-OPD nonzero mask, OPD restricted to the DeepScaleR RLVR nonzero mask, and OPD restricted to random mask with the same density as the full-OPD mask. The mask densities come directly from the complement of the sparsity measurements in Table 2: roughly 17.5% for the DS-Qwen OPD mask and random mask, and 22.1% for the DeepScaleR RLVR mask. Figure 3(a) shows that the discovered subnetwork is sufficient for recovering reasoning performance under OPD, using the average validation accuracy over AIME24 and AIME25. Full OPD reaches peak mean@16 accuracy of 35.52%, while the OPD-mask run reaches 35.10% and has slightly higher late-training average over the last five evaluation points (34.73% versus 34.27%). The RLVR mask also trains successfully, peaking at 34.69%, which is consistent with the overlap results above: the RLVR subnetwork is related to the OPD subnetwork, but it is not the same mask. The random mask improves over the source model but remains weaker, peaking at 32.92% and showing lower late-training average. Appendix A.9 shows the same subnetwork-masked pattern in the Qwen2.5-VL OPD setting. Thus, the sparse mask is not merely budget constraint; the particular coordinates selected by post-training matter. This makes the OPD delta sparse task vector in the sense of task arithmetic: the delta θfull θ0 is direction in weight space that implements behavioral change (Ilharco et al., 2022). Unlike classical lottery tickets, which are uncovered by pruning dense networks (Frankle & Carbin, 2018), the OPD task vector is naturally sparse at checkpoint precision. Masked training on the support of this sparse task vector is enough to recover full-training performance, suggesting that the visible OPD support is not only descriptive but operationally useful. 4.5 Do We Need AdamW in OPD? The second functional test asks whether OPD needs AdamW (Kingma & Ba, 2014; Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017), motivated by recent evidence that SGD can be surprisingly 7 Preprint. (a) Subnetwork-masked OPD (b) AdamW versus SGD Figure 3: Comparison of OPD reasoning performance along the training process, reported as average validation accuracy over AIME24 and AIME25. Left: full OPD versus OPD trained with the OPD, RLVR, or random masks. Right: AdamW versus SGD in the JustRL-teacher OPD setting. Benchmark-wise curves are shown in Appendix A.7. competitive for sparse RLVR updates (Mukherjee et al., 2026b). We run direct optimizer ablation in the JustRL-teacher OPD setting: AdamW with learning rate 106 versus vanilla SGD with learning rate 102. This follows prior optimizer analyses showing that AdamWs small nominal rate can correspond to much larger effective per-parameter rates after secondmoment normalization, so SGD is evaluated with larger unnormalized learning rate rather than the same nominal value (Mukherjee et al., 2026b). Figure 3(b) shows that SGD underperforms AdamW on the same reasoning validation suite, averaging mean@16 accuracy over AIME24 and AIME25. AdamW reaches 43.02% peak and 42.40% final mean@16 accuracy, while SGD reaches 39.06% peak and 37.92% final accuracy. Thus, the fact that OPD produces sparse final deltas is not sufficient to carry over the conclusion from RLVR that AdamWs momentum and adaptive learning rates are largely unnecessary. likely reason is that OPD changes the learning signal, which in turn changes the optimization dynamics. Mukherjee et al. (2026b) argue for SGD in RLVR by showing that AdamWs momentum can become poorly aligned with current gradients and that adaptive second-moment scaling is less load-bearing in their RLVR setting. For OPD, the distillation objective can produce different gradient geometry: every generated token receives supervision from the same teacher rather than only sparse and noisy reward signal. Appendix A.8 provides supporting diagnostics for this interpretation in the JustRL-teacher AdamW run. Momentum alignment is relatively high but unstable: it starts at 0.958, remains positive on average (0.280), drops to 0.008 by the end, and briefly becomes negative. This suggests that AdamWs first moment is not simply uninformative in OPD, even though its fluctuating alignment makes momentum an incomplete explanation by itself. More importantly, the second-moment coefficient of variation remains large, averaging 4.85, suggesting that AdamWs coordinate-wise scaling is still useful for OPD. In short, OPD shares sparse final-update structure with on-policy post-training, but its dense teacher signal can preserve enough gradient heterogeneity for AdamW to matter.

sections.title: 5 Geometry of OPD Updates
sections.content: 5.1 Full-Rank but Spectrally Concentrated OPD updates are not low-rank in the strict numerical sense. Figure 1 and Table 2 summarize the main spectral evidence, with full spectral statistics in Appendix Table 9. For the six OPDstyle pairs, the median numerical rank percentage is 100% under the relative singular-value threshold σi > 105σ1. However, this full-rank property coexists with spectral concentration. Their median top-16 singular-value energy ratio ranges from 19.69% to 40.94%. The corresponding median stable ranks range from 7.25 to 20.31. The offline distillation contrast 8 Preprint. is also numerically full-rank, but its spectrum is much more diffuse: Qwen-Math Distill has only 8.57% top-16 energy and stable rank 82.74. By contrast, DeepScaleR RLVR is closer to OPD (19.62% top-16 energy and stable rank 16.07), suggesting that spectral concentration is more characteristic of sparse on-policy post-training than of offline distillation. This distinction matters for interpreting low-rank adaptation. If one only reports numerical rank, OPD appears incompatible with low-rank parameterization. If one only reports top-k energy, OPD may appear effectively low-dimensional. more accurate statement lies between these extremes: OPD deltas are full-rank matrices with spectrally concentrated energy profile. Low-rank methods may therefore capture meaningful part of the update energy, but they should not be expected to exactly reproduce the full checkpoint delta. 5.2 Off-Principal Movement from Source Weights OPD updates have weak alignment with the principal singular directions of the source weights. For each source matrix Wsrc = UΣV, we measure WVk2 W2 , (3) where Uk and Vk are the leading left and right singular vectors of Wsrc. The main coverage scores are reported in Table 2, and the 1%, 5%, 10%, and 20% source-mask variants are given in Appendix Table 10. At rank fraction of 10%, the median joint principal projection is below 1% for every main OPD-style pair. This means that OPD is not primarily amplifying the high-energy directions already dominant in the source weights. The offline distillationstyle contrast shows weaker version of this effect: Qwen-Math Distill has 2.22% median principal-projection energy, several times larger than DS-Qwen OPD and DeepScaleR RLVR. The coordinate-mask analysis gives the same conclusion in more local form. At the 10% mask setting, source-principal coordinates cover only 4.39% to 5.46% of visible OPD update coordinates, roughly half of the 10% random-density baseline. By contrast, the bottom 10% source-magnitude coordinates cover 24.99% to 48.57% of visible OPD update coordinates. The distillation-style contrast is close to random on both masks, with 9.95% principal coverage and 10.10% low-magnitude coverage. DeepScaleR RLVR shows the same qualitative bias as OPD, with 5.29% principal and 31.26% low-magnitude coverage. This contrast strengthens the interpretation that off-principal, low-magnitude writing is signature of on-policy training; it also marks where OPD differs from offline distillation.

sections.title: 6 Discussion and Future Work
sections.content: The primary message of this paper is that OPD produces small, sparse, spectrally concentrated, off-principal updates. Rather than interpolating linearly between SFT and RLVR in parameter space, OPD remains closer to sparse on-policy post-training. qualitative explanation is that OPD trains the model near its own behavioral distribution. Unlike offline distillation, which may force the student to fit external trajectories far from what it currently generates, OPD provides teacher feedback on the students own rollouts. The student can therefore refine behavior in familiar region of its policy space rather than broadly rewriting parameters to imitate off-policy traces. This offers one reason why dense token-level supervision can still produce such updates. Our experiments mainly study OPD through static final-checkpoint analysis. However, we do not trace the full OPD training trajectory, so the learning dynamics that produce the final task vector remain to be revealed. More broadly, this paper focuses on how OPD changes model in weight space; complementary behavioral analyses of how on-policy training changes model outputs remain an important direction (Shenfeld et al., 2025). Another future direction concerns scale and coverage. Our observational experiments include relatively small-scale LLM and VLM checkpoints; interventional experiments are limited to DS-Qwen and Qwen2.5-VL for math reasoning. Larger models, additional domains such as agentic or embodied tasks, and more OPD applications are needed to 9 Preprint. determine whether the observed sparsity, module structure, off-principal movement, and optimizer sensitivity are stable properties of OPD or artifacts of the present training recipe. third direction is OPD-native adaptation and optimization. The spectral concentration of OPD updates naturally suggests low-rank adaptation methods such as LoRA and its variants (Hu et al., 2021; Schulman & Lab, 2025), while the off-principal geometry suggests orthogonal fine-tuning methods (Liu et al., 2024). On the optimizer side, OPDs matrix-level geometry may be incompatible with the spectral normalization introduced in vanilla Muon, making recent high-pass variants worth testing (Jordan et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2025; Fan et al., 2026). More broadly, these results point toward OPD-native fine-tuning methods and optimizers that allocate capacity according to the sparse, spectrally concentrated, offprincipal task vector induced by on-policy distillation.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: This work studies OPD parameter updates through two complementary properties: sparsity and geometry. Observational studies show that OPD-style updates are small, coordinatesparse, spectrally concentrated but numerically full-rank, and strongly biased away from source-principal coordinates toward low-magnitude source coordinates. Interventional results show both the utility and the limits of this structure: training only the discovered OPD subnetwork is sufficient to recover full-OPD reasoning performance, but vanilla SGD underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation. Together, these findings place OPD closer to sparse on-policy editing than to dense supervised rewriting, even though its learning signal is dense and teacher-derived.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: A.1 Related Work On-Policy Distillation. Early OPD formulations emphasize the exposure-mismatch problem in offline sequence-level distillation and train the student on its own sampled trajectories with dense teacher feedback (Agarwal et al., 2024; Gu et al., 2024). Recent work shows that this idea has become practical post-training component for large language models (Song & Zheng, 2026), including large-to-small distillation (Yang et al., 2025; Li et al., 2026), capability consolidation with multiple teachers or post-training stages (Xiao et al., 2026; Zeng et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2026; DeepSeek-AI, 2026; OpenBMB, 2026), and on-policy self-distillation from privileged information (Zhao et al., 2026; ubotter et al., 2026; Ye et al., 2026). Our work differs from these papers by studying the parameter update induced by OPD, rather than proposing new OPD objective or training recipe. Parameter Geometry of Post-Training. second line of related work analyzes the parameter geometry of post-training. Mukherjee et al. (2026a) show that RLVR fine-tunes small subnetworks in large language models, while Zhu et al. (2025) argue that RLVR learns away from the principal directions of source weights. Cai et al. (2025) further study reinforcementlearning-induced parameter dynamics and find that dominant low-rank directions can predict later reasoning improvements. Mukherjee et al. (2026b) connect this sparse update regime to optimizer choice, showing that SGD can be surprisingly competitive for RLVR. We ask whether these properties persist when the sparse reward signal is replaced by dense teacher supervision, which places OPD between conventional dense-supervised fine-tuning and sparse-reward on-policy training. Our AdamW-versus-SGD result shows that the static sparsity analogy does not automatically carry over to optimizer choice. Adaptation and Optimization Methods. Finally, the spectral and off-principal results relate to parameter-efficient adaptation and optimizer design. Low-rank adaptation methods such as LORA exploit the concentration of fine-tuning updates in low-dimensional additive subspace (Hu et al., 2021; Schulman & Lab, 2025). Orthogonal fine-tuning methods such as OFT/BOFT instead parameterize adaptation through orthogonal transformations, aiming to preserve hyperspherical energy and pretrained representation structure while changing the models function (Liu et al., 2024). Muon is relevant from the optimizer side because it treats hidden-layer weights as matrices and orthogonalizes the momentum update before applying it (Jordan et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2025). Recent work argues that this spectral normalization can fail in RLVR post-training and proposes high-pass remedies, making these variants more plausible starting point for OPD than vanilla Muon (Fan et al., 2026). These methods motivate future OPD-native optimization strategies. A.2 Post-Training Objectives for LLMs We summarize the training objectives that define the regimes compared in this paper. Let denote prompt, = (y1, . . . , yT) response, πθ the trainable policy, πT teacher policy, and πref reference policy used for KL regularization when applicable. SFT and Sequence-Level KD. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) optimizes the negative loglikelihood of fixed target responses: LSFT(θ) = (x,y)D t=1 log πθ(y x, <t). (4) Sequence-level knowledge distillation (SEQKD) uses the same maximum-likelihood objective, but the target response yT is generated by teacher rather than drawn from human annotations (Hinton et al., 2015; Kim & Rush, 2016): LSeqKD(θ) = xD, yT πT (x) t= log πθ(yT x, yT <t). (5) 14 Preprint. Both objectives are dense in the sense that every target token supplies supervised learning signal, but the trajectories are offline-collected and fixed before the student update. RLVR and GRPO. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) samples responses from the current or recent policy and optimizes scalar reward r(x, y), often with KL penalty to keep the policy close to πref: JRLVR(θ) = xD, yπθ (x) r(x, y) β (cid:34) t=1 DKL(πθ(x, y<t) πref(x, y<t)) . (6) (cid:35) GRPO is seminal RLVR method that removes the learned critic by sampling group of responses {yi}G i=1 for the same prompt and normalizing their rewards within the group (Shao et al., 2024). With ˆAi = (ri mean({rj}G j=1) + ϵ) and ρi,t(θ) = πθ(yi,tx, yi,<t)/πold(yi,tx, yi,<t), clipped GRPO objective can be written as (cid:34) j=1))/(std({rj}G (cid:35) JGRPO(θ) = i=1 x,{yi}G 1 G i=1 yi t=1 ρi,t(θ) ˆAi, clip(ρi,t(θ), 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ) ˆAi [gi,t(θ) βki,t(θ)] 1 yi (cid:16) gi,t(θ) = min ki,t(θ) = DKL(πθ(x, yi,<t) πref(x, yi,<t)) . , (cid:17) , (7) RLVR therefore uses on-policy or near-on-policy samples but usually receives sparse sequence-level reward feedback. OPD. OPD combines on-policy student rollouts with dense teacher feedback. One style, which we call GKD-style OPD, applies token-level teacher-student divergence on prefixes generated by the student (Agarwal et al., 2024): LOPD-GKD(θ) = xD, yπθ (x) t=1 D(πT(x, y<t) πθ(x, y<t)) , (8) where is typically an -divergence over token distributions. Another style, which we call PG-style OPD, treats distillation as policy optimization on student-generated samples (Gu et al., 2024). An abstract form is the reverse-KL sequence objective LOPD-PG(θ) = xD, yπθ (x) t=1 [log πθ(ytx, y<t) log πT(ytx, y<t)] , (9) which can be optimized with policy-gradient estimators because the sampled trajectory depends on the student policy. This PG view is connected to reverse-KL distillation through simple score-function identity. For fixed prefix = (x, y<t), define the sampled-token estimator k1(a; c) = log πθ(ac) log πT(ac). The full-vocabulary reverse KL at this prefix is DKL(πθ(c) πT(c)) = πθ(ac)k1(a; c). Its gradient is θ DKL(πθ(c) πT(c)) = θ πθ(ac)k1(a; c) [θπθ(ac)] k1(a; c) + aπθ (c) [k1(a; c)θ log πθ(ac)] + aπθ (c) [k1(a; c)θ log πθ(ac)] . = = = πθ(ac)θk1(a; c) θπθ(ac) (10) (11) Here we use θπθ(ac) = πθ(ac)θ log πθ(ac) and θk1(a; c) = θ log πθ(ac), since the prefix and the teacher πT are fixed. The final equality uses πθ(ac) = 1, so 15 Preprint. θπθ(ac) = θ1 = 0. Thus, if k1 is detached and k1(a; c) is used as the policygradient advantage, the score-function loss LPG(a; c) = sg[k1(a; c)] log πθ(ac) has gradient k1(a; c)θ log πθ(ac), giving an unbiased single-sample estimator of the reverse-KL gradient up to practical modifications such as clipping, off-policy importance ratios, and advantage normalization. Both OPD styles differ from SEQKD by training on student-generated trajectories, and differ from RLVR by replacing sparse scalar rewards with dense teacher-derived feedback. A.3 Model Pairs and Training Details Table 7 lists the checkpoint pairs used in the delta analysis. These pairs cover LLMs with PG-style OPD updates and VLMs with GKD-style OPD updates, and they span the three use cases of OPD discussed in Section A.1. For training and evaluation, we use the public implementations and original configurations from verl/HybridFlow (Sheng et al., 2025), the OPD codebase, and the OPSD codebase where applicable.2 The DS-Qwen OPD runs use DAPO-Math-17K (Yu et al., 2026), the VLM GRPO and OPD runs use Geo3K (Lu et al., 2021), the Qwen3-1.7B OPD pair is public checkpoint distilled from Qwen3-4B-Base-GRPO, and the OPSD run uses subsets of OpenThoughts math 30k (Guha et al., 2025). For each 2D update matrix, we analyze the singular values of the update: = UΣV , Σ = diag(σ1, . . . , σm), σ1 σm 0. The top-k spectral energy ratio is ek(W) = i=1 σ2 i=1 σ2 + ϵ0 . (20) Stable rank is continuous proxy for rank based on energy spread: it is close to 1 when the top singular value dominates and increases as energy spreads across many singular directions. Numerical rank at tolerance τ is rankτ(W) = {i : σi > τσ1}. Numerical rank counts how many singular values remain above relative threshold; lower values indicate more sharply truncated spectrum at that tolerance. Taken together, higher top-k energy and lower stable or numerical rank indicate stronger spectral concentration. Spectral concentration means that the update is directionally anisotropic and can be well approximated, in Frobenius energy, by small number of rank-one singular components; it does not imply that the matrix is exactly low-rank or that the update is functionally unimportant. These quantities separate two notions that are often conflated: an update can be numerically full-rank while still having much of its energy concentrated in small number of singular directions. The implementation also computes left-only and right-only sourceprincipal projections, source-subspace rotation, and source spectral drift; the tables in this paper report the joint projection because it is the strictest alignment measure. A.7 Benchmark-Wise Breakdown of Interventional Experiments The benchmark-wise curves support the same conclusions as the averaged curves in Figure 3. For the subnetwork intervention, the OPD mask closely tracks full training on both AIME24 and AIME25, while the random-mask run generally performs worse. For the optimizer ablation, SGD improves over the source checkpoint but trails AdamW on both benchmarks by the end of training, indicating that the sparse final-update structure does not by itself make AdamW unnecessary. A.8 Optimizer Dynamics in OPD We further inspect the AdamW optimizer state in the JustRL-teacher OPD run, following the same diagnostic perspective used by Mukherjee et al. (2026b). We use two optimizer-state metrics. First, momentum cosine similarity is computed as cos(mt, gt) = mt, gt mt2gt2 , where mt is AdamWs first-moment estimate and gt is the current gradient after flattening the trainable parameters. Values close to 1 mean that the accumulated momentum points in the current descent direction, values near 0 mean that the first moment carries little directional agreement with the current gradient, and negative values mean that momentum is locally opposed to the current gradient. The momentum cosine is relatively high but unstable: it starts at 0.958, remains positive on average with mean 0.280, falls toward zero, and reaches minimum of 0.351. This persistent heterogeneity suggests that AdamWs coordinate-wise adaptive scaling can still be load-bearing in OPD, even when the final checkpoint delta is sparse. A.9 Qwen2.5-VL Subnetwork Experiments We repeat the masked-subnetwork intervention in the Qwen2.5-VL OPD setting. All runs use the same Geo3K training configuration and are evaluated on the Geo3K validation set, reported as accuracy over the first 100 optimizer steps. We compare five variants: full 21 Preprint. OPD, OPD restricted to the full-OPD nonzero mask, OPD restricted to the GRPO nonzero mask, OPD restricted to random mask with the same density as the full-OPD mask, and OPD restricted to random mask with the same density as the GRPO mask. The nonzero masks are again defined by > 105; the full-OPD mask has roughly one-third active coordinates, while the GRPO mask has 9.3% active coordinates. Figure 7: Qwen2.5-VL subnetwork-masked OPD on Geo3K. The full-OPD mask closely tracks the full run, while density-matched random masks and the smaller GRPO mask underperform. Figure 7 shows the same qualitative pattern as the LLM subnetwork intervention. Full OPD reaches peak Geo3K accuracy of 55.72% and final-step accuracy of 54.94%, while the full-OPD-mask run reaches 54.24% peak and 54.23% final accuracy. The late-training gap is similarly small: averaged over the last five evaluation points, full OPD obtains 54.65%, compared with 53.86% for the full-OPD mask. The random mask with the same density as the full-OPD mask peaks lower at 52.30%, showing that the full-OPD support is not merely random one-third training budget. The GRPO mask reaches 49.98%, and the density-matched random GRPO mask peaks at 47.40%. Thus, in the VLM setting as well, the sparse coordinates selected by OPD are functionally useful for recovering most of the full-OPD improvement.
